source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Ch.3 Current Electricity (§3.13 Kirchhoff's Rules — the loop/voltage rule half) — table-of-contents + scope reference only; teaching authored from first principles. DC Pandey consulted for chapter SCOPE only (Current Electricity ToC places Kirchhoff's rules after combination_of_resistors/emf/internal_resistance/electrical_power, before Wheatstone/potentiometer) to confirm the atomic boundary — no teaching method, example problem, or figure imported. The atomic idea: around any closed loop in a circuit, the algebraic sum of potential changes is zero — ΣV = 0 — because the EMF raises the potential (+ε, a rise) and each resistor drops it (−IR, a drop along the current), so the rise always equals the total of the drops and a charge that travels the loop returns to exactly the potential it started at (energy conservation); for an ideal single-loop cell, ε = IR1 + IR2. The signs are the crux — traverse a source low-to-high is a RISE (+ε), traverse a resistor along the current is a DROP (−IR). Does NOT cover the junction rule / KCL (shipped, sibling `kirchhoff_junction_rule_KCL`), solving a multi-loop network by simultaneous KVL+KCL (mesh analysis, downstream application), internal resistance as a taught idea (its own shipped diamond `internal_resistance`; r never appears here as a guided-state target), a second/opposing EMF in the same loop (sign of ε, a natural drill-down candidate, deferred), or Wheatstone/potentiometer (downstream catalog nodes that depend on this one). NINTH Ch.3 concept on the particle_field (2D p5.js) renderer, same engine family as drift_velocity/ohms_law/resistivity/combination_of_resistors/emf_definition/internal_resistance/electrical_power_in_resistor/kirchhoff_junction_rule_KCL. Reuses scenario_type 'emf_definition' as the renderer selector (per the approved design's directive to NOT mint a new scenario_type) — but rides engine additions that are AUTHORED here and FLAGGED for peter_parker:renderer_primistives (via quality_auditor FAIL-routing), not yet implemented as of this authoring pass: (1) a multi-step series-resistor potential ladder (2-3 down-steps at R1/R2[/R3] instead of the single-resistor 'load' step the emf/internal_resistance family currently draws — extend drawPotentialLadder / the loop profile in particle_field_renderer.ts, gated behind the new per-state flag kvl_multi_ladder so emf_definition.json/internal_resistance.json render pixel-identically when the flag is absent); (2) per-element voltmeters (cell epsilon + each resistor, labeled+signed) by calling the EXISTING drawVoltmeterC primitive multiple times per state, gated behind show_element_voltmeters; (3) a kvl_sum_readout value HUD (sibling of the shipped kcl_sum_readout), a NEW draw block inside drawEmfScenario() rendering the SIGNED identity '+6.0 − 4.0 − 2.0 = 0.0' (not the unsigned sum kcl_sum_readout computes); (4) wiring the ALREADY-SHIPPED ghost_text/ghost_pos + drawStruckTextC mechanism (currently read only inside drawCircuit()) into drawEmfScenario() too, so STATE_3's fixed naive-expectation string '6 + 4 + 2 = 12' can render on the emf-family scenario; (5) new per-state cue-gated reveals (r2_autosweep 1→4Ω on STATE_3, r3_draw_in on STATE_4) mirroring the EXISTING r2_autosweep/r_reveal patterns already used by combination_of_resistors/internal_resistance, extended to the emf-family adapter; (6) a staged ladder-segment reveal on STATE_2 (ladder_build_ms array) — no existing precedent draws a ladder incrementally; (7) small fixed H/L sign-tag labels at each resistor's current-entry/exit ends (show_hl_tags, S3+). Zero regression declared to emf_definition/internal_resistance/combination_of_resistors/electrical_power_in_resistor/kirchhoff_junction_rule_KCL — every new flag is opt-in and absent from all five sibling JSONs. Conceptual-only (no mode_overrides), EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches) — the two genuine misconceptions ('leftover voltage after the drops', 'add all voltages regardless of direction') are confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch, fired together at the STATE_3 pivot only, no predict-pause. Anchors authored UNIVERSAL per Rule 35 (a hiking loop trail; a series lamp loop — no country, brand, festival, or currency; plain English throughout, no Hinglish).

real_world_anchor.primary: A hiking loop trail: climb the ridge, walk down the far side, and you arrive back at the trailhead at exactly the altitude you started — no matter how the descent splits between the two sides, the total descent always equals the climb. Around an electrical loop it's the same round trip: the cell is the climb, each resistor is a descent, and the total of the descents always equals the climb, however the descent is split.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A battery driving two lamps wired one after another in a single loop: the full push the battery delivers is shared between the two lamps, and nothing is left over once both are crossed.

emf.name: EMF (ideal cell, ε)
emf.role: locked via per-state numeric override on S1-S4 (Rule 25d — never a guided teaching target); live ONLY in S5 (explore) via the emf slider

R1.name: Resistor R1 (series)
R1.role: locked at 2Ω on S1/S2/S4; unchanged on S3 (R2 is the CAUSE that moves, R1 stays fixed) but its slider is visible+live post-reveal alongside R2; live-draggable S5

R2.name: Resistor R2 (series)
R2.role: locked at 1Ω on S1/S2/S4; S3 = scripted cue r2_autosweep sweeping 1Ω→4Ω (the CAUSE, Rule 32a) starting ~1000ms into the state, then live-draggable post-reveal; live S5

R3.name: Resistor R3 (series, third element)
R3.role: inert/undrawn on S1-S3 (three_resistor:false); drawn via the r3_draw_in cue (~900ms into STATE_4) and locked to 3Ω on S4; live-draggable ONLY in S5

I.name: Loop current (single value, series loop)
I.role: the SAME single number everywhere in the loop — no split (the sibling contrast to KCL's parallel branches); drives bead speed and the walking marker

V1.name: Drop across R1
V1.role: live readout — the voltmeter across R1; drives one down-step of the ladder. 4.0V on S1/S2, falls to 2.0V on S3, 2.0V on S4

V2.name: Drop across R2
V2.role: live readout — the voltmeter across R2; drives one down-step of the ladder. 2.0V on S1/S2, rises to 4.0V on S3, 1.0V on S4

V3.name: Drop across R3
V3.role: live readout — the voltmeter across R3; only rendered when three_resistor is true — 3.0V on S4

kvl_sum.name: Signed loop sum (the taught identity)
kvl_sum.role: MUST equal 0.0 at every instant, every state, every slider setting — this IS the taught rule, not a coincidence to verify; drives the kvl_sum_readout HUD and the ladder's closing-back-to-baseline

formulas.loop_equation: emf = I*R1 + I*R2 (+ I*R3) — the unsigned rise-equals-drops form taught on S2-S3; ΣV=0 is the equivalent signed form taught from S4 onward
formulas.kvl_identity: emf - V1 - V2 (- V3) === 0 identically, by construction on this ideal-cell series loop — this IS the taught rule (ΣV=0), not a coincidence to verify

constraints: ΣV = 0 EXACTLY at every setting — the definition of the taught rule, not a coincidence. V1 + V2 (+ V3) = emf always.
S3's R2 change REDISTRIBUTES the individual drops (V1 falls 4→2, V2 rises 2→4) but the SIGNED SUM never changes — Rule 32b's deliberate invariant.
I is the SAME single number everywhere in the loop (a series loop — no split; the sibling contrast to KCL's parallel branches).
R ∈ [1,8]Ω, emf ∈ [3,12]V ⇒ I ∈ [0.375,6]A — no divide-by-zero, no negative resistance, no infinite current.
S3's ghost '6 + 4 + 2 = 12' is a FIXED authored string pinned to the ORIGINAL 4/2 split — never recomputed, never allowed to equal the live signed sum.

H/L sign tags (a rise is low-to-high at the source; a drop is high-to-low across a resistor, along the current) are fixed geometry appearing S3+ — only the MAGNITUDES respond to sliders, never the sign convention itself.
emf is NEVER a live slider in guided states (locked via per-state numeric override); live only in S5 (explore).
R3 the ELEMENT is not rendered before STATE_4 (Rule 32d — STATE_4 only ADDS R3 to the same apparatus, no teleport-rebuild).
the optional internal-resistance slider r is EXCLUDED from S5 this ship — would force emf = I·ΣR to become approximate and needs a second engine capability (internal_resistance owns that idea).

aha_moment.statement: Change a resistor and the drops swap, but the signed sum always closes at zero.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: The ladder always lands at exactly zero height, while the naive ghost '6 + 4 + 2 = 12' hangs above it, struck through.

scene_composition.text: A closed loop is like a hiking trail: climb up, come back down, land at the same height.
A marker walks the loop: up at the cell, down across each resistor.
It always closes back to exactly where it started.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch a marker walk this loop: up at the cell, down across resistor R one, down again across resistor R two.
It lands back exactly where it started — a full round trip always returns to the same potential.

STATE_2.title: Rise equals total drops: ε = IR1 + IR2

scene_composition.text: The cell lifts the potential — a measurable rise up the ladder.
Voltmeters settle at six, then four, then two volts as the steps land.
Six volts up equals four plus two volts down.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now watch the numbers: the cell lifts the potential by six volts.
Crossing resistor R one drops it four volts, then resistor R two drops it another two volts.
Six volts up equals four plus two volts down — the rise always equals the total of the drops.

STATE_3.title: PRIMARY aha — signs close the loop, no leftover

scene_composition.text: Resistor R two grows from one ohm to four ohms — resistor R one stays fixed.
The drops swap: R one now reads two volts, R two reads four volts.
Adding all three as positive gives twelve volts — wrong. The signed sum is zero.

tts_sentences.text_en: Grow resistor R two from one ohm to four ohms; R one stays at two ohms.
The current falls and the drops swap: R one now drops two volts, R two drops four.
Adding all three as positive gives twelve volts, which is wrong. The signed sum, six minus four minus two, equals zero.

misconception_watch.belief: There's leftover voltage after the drops.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The ladder is pinned to exactly zero height at loop-close while the drops redistribute under the slider — nothing is ever left over, whatever the resistor values.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The rise equals the total of the drops — a full loop always nets to zero.

misconception_watch.belief: You add all voltages regardless of direction (signs).
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A struck-through runaway ghost '6 + 4 + 2 = 12' sits beside the real signed ladder closing to zero; the H/L tags anchor why the resistor terms are negative when traversed along the current.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Rises are plus, drops are minus (low to high at the source) — only the signed sum closes the loop.

STATE_4.title: Generalization: any loop, any number of elements

scene_composition.text: A third resistor, R three, joins the same loop.
The ladder now steps down three times and still lands at zero.
The sum of every voltage around any closed loop equals zero.

tts_sentences.text_en: Add a third resistor, R three, in series with the same loop.
The current settles lower, and the ladder now steps down three times: two volts, then one volt, then three volts.
Six volts up still equals two plus one plus three down — the sum of all voltages around any loop equals zero.

STATE_5.title: Explore — every dial is yours: ε, R1, R2, R3

scene_composition.text: Every dial is live — the cell's voltage and every resistor.
The ladder, every voltmeter, and the sum all respond as you drag.
The loop rule holds at every setting you choose.

tts_sentences.text_en: Every dial is yours now — drag the cell's voltage or any resistor and watch the loop always close to zero.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered Kirchhoff's loop rule when they can (1) state that a full round trip around any closed loop returns to the same potential, so the net change over the loop is exactly zero (energy conservation); (2) apply ε = IR1 + IR2 (or with more resistors) to find the current and each resistor's drop in a single-loop series circuit; (3) recognize that changing one resistor's value redistributes the individual drops but never changes their signed sum, which stays pinned at zero; (4) apply the correct sign convention — a source traversed low-to-high is a RISE (plus), a resistor traversed along the current is a DROP (minus) — rather than adding every element's voltage as a positive number; and (5) generalize ΣV = 0 to a loop with any number of elements, explaining WHY it holds — because potential is a property of position, not of the path taken to reach it.

questions.stem: In a simple loop with one ideal cell and two resistors, a positive test charge is walked once around the loop back to its starting point. What is the net change in potential over the full round trip?

options.A: Zero — the charge returns to exactly the potential it started at, since the rise at the cell exactly matches the total drops across the resistors
options.B: Positive — some potential is left over after crossing both resistors
options.C: Negative — the resistors take more than the cell supplies
options.D: It depends on which resistor the charge crosses first

distractor_misconceptions.B: the leftover-voltage misconception — imagines the round trip ending above where it started
distractor_misconceptions.C: assumes drops habitually exceed the rise, which would violate energy conservation
distractor_misconceptions.D: wrongly makes a globally conserved quantity depend on the order elements are crossed within the same loop

questions.tested_idea: A closed loop's net potential change is exactly zero (energy conservation) — the rise matches the total drops.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A charge circles a loop containing one battery and three resistors. State the net potential change for the full round trip, and explain why.

questions.stem: An ideal 6 V cell drives current through two series resistors, R1 = 2 Ω and R2 = 1 Ω. What do the voltmeters across R1 and R2 read, and how do those readings relate to the cell's 6 V?

options.A: V1 = 4 V, V2 = 2 V, and 4 + 2 = 6 V — the drops sum to exactly the rise
options.B: V1 = V2 = 3 V each, since two resistors always split a cell's voltage equally
options.C: V1 = 4 V, V2 = 2 V, but they don't need to add up to 6 V
options.D: V1 = 6 V, V2 = 6 V — every resistor in the loop sees the full cell voltage

distractor_misconceptions.B: assumes an automatic equal split between series resistors, ignoring their different resistances
distractor_misconceptions.C: gets the individual drop values right but drops the identity that they MUST sum to the rise
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses series drops (which share the total rise) with parallel branches (each of which sees the full voltage)

questions.tested_idea: In series, IR drops are proportional to each resistance and sum to exactly the EMF (ε = IR1 + IR2).
questions.parallel_form_stem: An ideal 12 V cell drives a 4 Ω and a 2 Ω resistor in series. Find each drop and confirm they sum to 12 V.

questions.stem: In the same loop, R2 is changed from 1 Ω to 4 Ω while R1 stays at 2 Ω. After the current settles, what is the sum of the two resistor drops?

options.A: Still exactly 6 V — the drops redistribute (V1 falls to 2 V, V2 rises to 4 V) but always sum to the cell's 6 V
options.B: 12 V — since R2 quadrupled, both drops should increase and add up higher
options.C: Less than 6 V — some voltage is lost adjusting to the new resistance
options.D: 6 V, but only because the current also increased to compensate

distractor_misconceptions.B: the naive add-all-positive trap — mistakes 'a resistor changed' for 'the total changed', ignoring that the current also FALLS
distractor_misconceptions.C: reintroduces the leftover/lost-voltage misconception at a new resistor setting
distractor_misconceptions.D: gets the sum right for the wrong reason — the current actually FALLS from 2 A to 1 A, it does not increase

questions.tested_idea: Changing one resistor redistributes the individual drops, but the SIGNED sum around the loop is invariant at ΣV = 0.
questions.parallel_form_stem: In a loop with an ideal cell and two series resistors, one resistor's value is increased. Explain what happens to the total of the two drops.

questions.stem: A student writes the loop equation as '6 + 4 + 2 = 12 V' by adding all three element voltages as positive numbers. What is wrong with this equation?

options.A: The resistor drops must be SUBTRACTED, not added — the correct signed equation is +6 − 4 − 2 = 0, because the cell raises the potential while each resistor lowers it
options.B: Nothing is wrong — 12 V is a valid alternate way to state the loop rule
options.C: The numbers themselves are wrong; the drops should have been 3 V and 3 V instead
options.D: The equation is right, but the units should be amperes, not volts

distractor_misconceptions.B: accepts the add-all-positive error as an equally valid form — it is not; only the signed sum equals zero
distractor_misconceptions.C: misdirects the error onto the numeric values instead of the sign convention
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses volts and amperes — a category error unrelated to the actual mistake

questions.tested_idea: The loop rule requires signed traversal (rises positive, drops negative along the current) — only the signed sum equals zero.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A student sums a cell's EMF and two resistor drops all as positive numbers and gets a nonzero total. Identify and correct the error.

questions.stem: A third resistor R3 is added in series to the loop (now three resistors in series with the ideal cell). What must be true of ΣV around this new, larger loop?

options.A: ΣV = 0 still — the rise at the cell equals the sum of all three drops, however many resistors are in the loop
options.B: ΣV = 0 only for loops with exactly two resistors; a third resistor needs a different rule
options.C: ΣV becomes positive, since there's now more resistance to overcome
options.D: ΣV = 0 only if all three resistors happen to be equal

distractor_misconceptions.B: wrongly restricts the loop rule to a specific element count, when it is a general conservation statement
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents a resistance-dependent nonzero total, contradicting the rule
distractor_misconceptions.D: invents an unnecessary equal-values special case

questions.tested_idea: ΣV = 0 generalizes to any closed loop, with any number of elements, not just the two-resistor case.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A loop has an ideal cell and four resistors in series. State what must be true of the sum of potential changes around the loop.

questions.stem: Why does ΣV = 0 hold for a closed loop with any number and arrangement of elements, not just a specific circuit?

options.A: Because potential is a property of position — after a full round trip you return to the same point, so the net change in potential must be zero regardless of the path taken to get there (energy conservation)
options.B: Because circuit designers arrange components to force the voltages to balance
options.C: Because Kirchhoff's loop rule only works for circuits with exactly one cell
options.D: Because resistors automatically adjust their own drop to make the sum work out

distractor_misconceptions.B: attributes a conservation law to deliberate engineering rather than physics
distractor_misconceptions.C: wrongly restricts the rule to single-cell loops — the underlying argument has no such limit
distractor_misconceptions.D: invents resistors as active agents 'adjusting' rather than passively obeying Ohm's law at whatever current actually flows

questions.tested_idea: ΣV = 0 follows from potential being a path-independent (conservative) quantity — a round trip always returns to the start.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Explain why Kirchhoff's loop rule works just as well on a five-element loop as it does on a two-resistor loop.

regeneration_variants.label: A hiking loop trail — climb up, come back down, no altitude left over
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A hiker climbs a ridge and walks back down the far side, arriving at the trailhead at exactly the altitude they started, however the descent splits between the two sides of the loop.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: A round trip through a conservative field (gravitational potential here, electric potential in the circuit) always nets to zero — the total climb equals the total descent, split however it splits.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Primary teaching anchor (real_world_anchor.primary) restated as a Type-B variant for STATE_1's round-trip beat.

regeneration_variants.label: A battery driving two lamps in series — the push is shared, nothing left over
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A battery and two lamps wired one after another in a single loop; the full push the battery delivers is shared between the two lamps, and nothing is left over once both are crossed.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same ΣV = 0 identity — the battery's rise is shared as drops across the two lamps in series, exactly like the sim's two resistors.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Secondary anchor restated as a Type-B variant for STATE_2/STATE_4's quantitative beats.
